_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against 2017 EULAR revised recommendations (Macfarlane), 2016 ACR diagnostic criteria revision (Wolfe), Clauw NEJM 2014 review, Sluka 2016 mechanistic review, and Hauser 2021 Lancet Rheumatology overview. Drug doses checked against FDA labels for duloxetine, milnacipran, and pregabalin.

h1Title: Fibromyalgia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Fibromyalgia is a chronic central sensitization disorder defined by widespread musculoskeletal pain, profound fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, and cognitive difficulty often called fibro fog. It affects roughly 2-4% of adults worldwide, with women diagnosed three to seven times more often than men and prevalence rising with age. Pain is generated by amplified processing in the central nervous system rather than tissue damage, so blood tests and scans usually return normal. Average time from first symptoms to diagnosis runs 2-3 years, and patients are commonly misdiagnosed with depression, hypothyroidism, or rheumatoid arthritis along the way. Multimodal treatment combining exercise, cognitive behavioral therapy, and targeted medication brings meaningful symptom control for most patients.

keyStats.demographics: Women 3-7x more affected than men; peak diagnosis age 35-55
keyStats.avgAge: Onset most common ages 30-50; rises through age 70
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 200 million people worldwide; under-diagnosed in low-resource settings

definition: Fibromyalgia (ICD-10: M79.7) is a chronic pain syndrome driven by altered nociceptive processing in the central nervous system — a mechanism now classified by the International Association for the Study of Pain as nociplastic pain, distinct from nociceptive pain (tissue injury) and neuropathic pain (nerve injury). Patients experience widespread musculoskeletal pain present for at least three months across multiple body regions, accompanied by fatigue, sleep disturbance, and cognitive symptoms. The 2016 American College of Rheumatology criteria define the diagnosis through two patient-completed scales: the Widespread Pain Index (WPI, 0-19) recording the number of painful body areas, and the Symptom Severity Scale (SSS, 0-12) capturing fatigue, sleep, cognitive symptoms, and somatic complaints. Diagnosis requires WPI of 7 or more with SSS of 5 or more, or WPI 4-6 with SSS 9 or more, plus generalized pain in at least four of five body regions and symptoms persisting at least three months. The 1990 tender-point exam has been retired by both ACR and EULAR. Pathophysiology centers on lowered nociceptive thresholds, reduced descending inhibition, altered functional connectivity in pain-processing networks, and HPA-axis dysregulation. Rheumatology manages most cases, frequently in collaboration with primary care, pain medicine, neurology, and behavioral health.

typesClassification.description: Fibromyalgia arising without an identifiable triggering disease. Accounts for roughly two-thirds of cases. Onset is often gradual, sometimes preceded by physical or psychological stressors.

Fibromyalgia coexisting with another rheumatic disease such as rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, or axial spondyloarthritis. Prevalence within those diseases reaches 20-30%, and central sensitization can drive symptoms even when joint disease is well-controlled.

Onset triggered by physical trauma, surgery, viral infection (including post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2), or major emotional stress. Symptoms typically begin within months of the inciting event and follow the same chronic course.

Pediatric form, more common in adolescent girls. Presents with widespread pain, sleep disturbance, and school dysfunction. Long-term follow-up shows persistence of symptoms into adulthood in roughly 50% of cases.

primarySymptoms: Widespread musculoskeletal pain present on both sides of the body, above and below the waist, lasting at least three months — described as deep aching, burning, or throbbing rather than sharp.
Profound fatigue that does not improve with rest, often disproportionate to activity, present on waking and worsening through the day in many patients.
Unrefreshing sleep — patients fall asleep but wake feeling unrested, with frequent awakenings and disrupted slow-wave sleep on polysomnography (alpha-delta intrusion).
Cognitive symptoms collectively known as fibro fog — difficulty with short-term memory, sustained attention, word-finding, and processing speed; reported by 50-80% of patients.
Morning stiffness lasting under an hour, distinct from the prolonged morning stiffness of inflammatory arthritis.
Tenderness to pressure over soft tissues including the neck, shoulders, low back, hips, and outer elbows — diffuse rather than focal.
Headaches, including tension-type and migraine, reported in 40-70% of patients.
Gastrointestinal symptoms overlapping with irritable bowel syndrome — alternating constipation and diarrhea, bloating, abdominal discomfort.
Numbness, tingling, or paresthesia in the hands and feet without an objective neurological deficit on examination.
Heightened sensitivity to light, sound, odors, temperature, and touch — known as multisensory hypersensitivity.
Pelvic pain, urinary urgency, and dysmenorrhea overlap with interstitial cystitis and chronic pelvic pain syndrome.
Mood symptoms — depression in roughly 30% and anxiety in 50% of patients, often as a consequence rather than a cause of chronic pain.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent fatigue and unrefreshing sleep developing over weeks to months without an obvious cause
Pain spreading from one area (such as low back or neck) to multiple body regions over time
Difficulty concentrating, losing words, or feeling mentally slow during routine tasks
Heightened sensitivity to light, noise, or temperature changes that previously felt normal
Symptoms emerging after physical trauma, surgery, viral illness, or major emotional stress
emergencySigns: New severe headache, fever, neck stiffness, or focal neurological deficit — these are not fibromyalgia and require urgent evaluation for infection, vascular, or structural disease
Sudden weight loss, night sweats, or persistent fever — workup for malignancy, infection, or inflammatory disease takes priority
Single hot, swollen, red joint — fibromyalgia does not cause objective joint inflammation; consider septic arthritis, crystal arthropathy, or rheumatoid flare
Acute suicidal ideation related to chronic pain — escalate to emergency mental-health services without delay

causes.cause: Central sensitization and amplified pain processing
causes.description: The core mechanism. Functional MRI shows heightened activity in pain-processing regions (insula, anterior cingulate, somatosensory cortex) and altered connectivity in the default mode network when patients receive painful stimuli. Pain thresholds are lowered and stimuli that should not hurt are perceived as painful (allodynia).

Healthy nervous systems dampen incoming pain signals through descending serotonergic and noradrenergic pathways. In fibromyalgia, this conditioned pain modulation is impaired, allowing pain signals to amplify. This mechanism explains why serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (duloxetine, milnacipran) are effective.

First-degree relatives of patients have an 8-fold higher risk. Twin studies estimate heritability at roughly 50%. Implicated genes include those involved in serotonin transport (SLC6A4), catecholamine metabolism (COMT), and dopamine signaling, though no single causal variant has been identified.

Symptoms commonly begin after a physical or psychological stressor — motor vehicle crash, surgery, viral illness (notably post-COVID), childbirth, sustained workplace stress, or bereavement. The stressor does not cause fibromyalgia in isolation but precipitates symptoms in predisposed individuals.

Polysomnography in fibromyalgia shows alpha-wave intrusion into delta (slow-wave) sleep. Experimental disruption of slow-wave sleep in healthy volunteers reproduces fibromyalgia-like pain and fatigue within days, suggesting sleep disturbance is both consequence and amplifier of the condition.

Cortisol response to stress is blunted in many patients, and autonomic testing shows reduced heart-rate variability and orthostatic intolerance in a subset. These changes are consistent with a stress-response system stuck in an altered baseline.

causes.cause: Small fiber neuropathy in a subset
causes.description: Skin biopsy studies show reduced intra-epidermal nerve fiber density in roughly 40-50% of fibromyalgia patients. Whether small fiber pathology is cause, consequence, or coincident is unresolved, but it may explain burning and paresthesia symptoms.

riskFactors.description: Women are diagnosed 3-7 times more frequently than men. Hormonal, immunological, and pain-perception differences contribute; diagnostic bias toward women may also inflate the gap.
Peak diagnosis is in midlife. Prevalence rises through age 70, then plateaus. New-onset fibromyalgia after age 75 is uncommon and should prompt broader workup.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of fibromyalgia or chronic pain
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives have an 8-fold higher risk. Heritability estimated at roughly 50% from twin studies.

Childhood physical, sexual, or emotional abuse and neglect roughly double adult fibromyalgia risk in cohort studies. Early-life stress appears to durably alter stress-response and pain-processing circuits.
20-30% of patients with rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, or axial spondyloarthritis meet criteria for concomitant fibromyalgia, complicating disease-activity assessment.

Depression and generalized anxiety increase fibromyalgia incidence in prospective cohorts. The relationship is bidirectional — chronic pain also raises depression risk — but treating mood symptoms improves pain outcomes.

riskFactors.factor: Sleep disorders, especially obstructive sleep apnea
riskFactors.description: OSA is present in roughly 30% of fibromyalgia patients in some series. Treating OSA reduces fatigue and pain in those affected.
Motor vehicle crashes, deployment-related trauma, surgery, and post-traumatic stress disorder all increase incidence in prospective studies.
BMI above 30 is associated with worse fibromyalgia symptoms and lower quality of life. Modest weight loss improves pain scores in intervention studies.
Low cardiorespiratory fitness predicts worse symptom severity. Graded aerobic exercise is one of the few interventions with strong evidence for symptom improvement.

affectedDemographics: US prevalence approximately 2% of adults overall — 3.4% in women and 0.5% in men (Wolfe 2016 cohort)
Worldwide prevalence ranges 1-5% depending on diagnostic criteria; the older 1990 ACR criteria yielded lower female-to-male ratios than the 2016 revision
Diagnosis is delayed by an average of 2-3 years from symptom onset; women of color and lower-income patients face longer delays in US studies
Children and adolescents account for a small but recognized share — juvenile fibromyalgia prevalence is roughly 2-6% in adolescent populations, peaking ages 13-15
Veterans and active-duty military personnel have elevated rates linked to physical trauma and post-traumatic stress disorder
diagnosisOverview: Fibromyalgia is a clinical diagnosis. There is no blood test, imaging study, or biopsy that confirms it; testing serves to exclude conditions that mimic the symptom cluster. Workup begins with a structured history capturing pain location, duration, sleep, fatigue, cognitive symptoms, and triggers, followed by a focused musculoskeletal and neurological examination. The diagnosis is made using the 2016 ACR criteria: a Widespread Pain Index (WPI) of 0-19 counting painful areas across 19 body regions and a Symptom Severity Scale (SSS, 0-12) rating fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive symptoms, and additional somatic complaints. A patient meets criteria when WPI is 7 or more with SSS 5 or more, or WPI 4-6 with SSS 9 or more, with generalized pain present in at least four of five body regions and symptoms lasting at least three months. The diagnosis can coexist with other rheumatic, neurological, or psychiatric conditions and should not be excluded simply because another disease is present. Routine laboratory exclusion typically includes a complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, thyroid-stimulating hormone, C-reactive protein, vitamin D, ferritin, and creatine kinase. Antinuclear antibody and rheumatoid factor are ordered when joint swelling, rash, or other inflammatory features are present, not as routine screening. Sleep history should screen for obstructive sleep apnea, and polysomnography is indicated when snoring, witnessed apneas, or excessive daytime sleepiness raise suspicion. Imaging is reserved for red-flag symptoms — focal neurological deficits, weight loss, fever, or asymmetric joint swelling. The 1990 ACR tender-point examination has been retired and should not be used to confirm or exclude diagnosis in current practice.

diagnosticTests.test: Widespread Pain Index and Symptom Severity Scale (2016 ACR criteria)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The diagnostic instrument itself. Quantifies painful body regions (WPI 0-19) and severity of fatigue, sleep, cognition, and somatic symptoms (SSS 0-12). Replaces the 1990 tender-point exam.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A patient-completed form, usually taking 5-10 minutes during the office visit. The clinician scores it on the spot and discusses the result with you.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes hypothyroidism, which can cause widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and cognitive slowing — symptoms that overlap closely with fibromyalgia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw with results back the same or next day. Normal TSH does not exclude fibromyalgia; abnormal TSH redirects diagnosis to thyroid disease.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count and comprehensive metabolic panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for anemia, infection, kidney dysfunction, and electrolyte abnormalities that could explain fatigue or pain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw; results same or next day.

diagnosticTests.test: C-reactive protein (CRP) and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for systemic inflammation. Fibromyalgia is typically associated with normal inflammatory markers; elevated CRP or ESR should redirect workup toward inflammatory or infectious disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood test, often drawn alongside other panels.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies vitamin D deficiency and iron deficiency, both of which can amplify fatigue and pain and are correctable.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Replacement is straightforward when deficiency is confirmed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Ordered selectively when joint swelling, rash, Raynaud phenomenon, oral ulcers, or other features raise concern for lupus or rheumatoid arthritis. Routine screening is not recommended because low-titer positive results are common in healthy adults and can mislead.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood test typically run with other autoimmune markers when ordered.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates for obstructive sleep apnea, restless legs syndrome, and periodic limb movement disorder in patients with snoring, witnessed apneas, or unrefreshing sleep beyond what fibromyalgia alone would explain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Overnight study in a sleep lab or, increasingly, a home sleep apnea test. Results guide whether CPAP or other targeted treatment is added.

diagnosticTests.purpose: MRI of the spine, electromyography, or skin biopsy for small fiber neuropathy are used only when history and examination raise specific concern. None are required for routine fibromyalgia diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Vary by test. Skin biopsy for small fiber assessment is a 3 mm punch under local anesthetic at a few sites on the leg, with results in 2-4 weeks.

treatmentOverview: Fibromyalgia treatment is multimodal, individualized, and lifelong. Both the 2017 revised EULAR recommendations and current US best-practice consensus put non-pharmacological measures as the foundation: patient education, graded aerobic and resistance exercise, cognitive behavioral therapy, and sleep optimization. Medications are added when non-pharmacological approaches alone do not bring adequate symptom control. The TEAM model (Treat, Educate, Activate, Motivate) summarizes the strategy. Three medications carry US FDA approval for fibromyalgia: duloxetine 30-60 mg daily (serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor), milnacipran 50 mg twice daily (SNRI), and pregabalin 150-450 mg per day divided (alpha-2-delta ligand). Amitriptyline 10-50 mg at bedtime is widely used off-label with strong evidence, as is low-dose cyclobenzaprine 5-30 mg at bedtime. Low-dose naltrexone 4.5 mg at bedtime is an emerging option with growing evidence in small randomized trials. NSAIDs and acetaminophen provide modest benefit and are reasonable for breakthrough musculoskeletal pain but do not address the central mechanism. Opioids are strongly discouraged — they worsen central sensitization and hyperalgesia, carry addiction and overdose risk, and have no evidence of long-term benefit in fibromyalgia. Tramadol is sometimes used with caution; data are mixed. Cannabinoids have limited and inconsistent evidence. Complementary approaches with credible randomized data include tai chi (Wang NEJM 2018 showed equivalence or superiority to aerobic exercise), yoga, mindfulness-based stress reduction, and balneotherapy. Treatment response is judged by symptom severity, functional status, sleep quality, and patient-reported quality of life rather than by laboratory markers. Most patients require dose adjustment and combination therapy over time, and a multidisciplinary team — rheumatologist, primary care, physical therapy, pain psychology — produces the best outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Duloxetine (30 mg daily for 1 week, then 60 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved SNRI for fibromyalgia. Boosts descending serotonergic and noradrenergic pain inhibition. Works best for pain and mood symptoms; less impact on sleep. Common side effects are nausea, dry mouth, and constipation, usually settling in 2-4 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 50% of patients achieve a 30% or greater reduction in pain; number needed to treat (NNT) for 30% pain reduction is approximately 8 (Cochrane review).

medicalTreatments.name: Milnacipran (12.5 mg day 1, titrate to 50 mg twice daily over 1 week)
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved SNRI with stronger noradrenergic action than duloxetine. Useful when duloxetine is intolerated. Side effects include nausea, hot flashes, hypertension; monitor blood pressure during titration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30% pain reduction in roughly 40-45% of patients vs 30% with placebo; NNT approximately 11.

medicalTreatments.name: Pregabalin (75 mg twice daily, titrate to 150-225 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved alpha-2-delta ligand. Reduces excitatory neurotransmitter release. Improves pain and sleep; less effect on fatigue. Major side effects are dizziness, somnolence, weight gain, and peripheral edema. Schedule V controlled substance in the US.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 50% pain reduction in roughly 25-30% of patients vs 15% with placebo; NNT approximately 11 for substantial benefit.

medicalTreatments.name: Amitriptyline (10 mg at bedtime, titrate to 25-50 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Tricyclic antidepressant used off-label with strong evidence in fibromyalgia. Improves sleep, pain, and fatigue. Anticholinergic side effects (dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention) and morning grogginess limit dose escalation, especially in older adults.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30% pain reduction in roughly 35-40% of patients; long-term benefit attenuates in about a third of responders.

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclobenzaprine (5-10 mg at bedtime, can increase to 30 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Structurally related to tricyclics. Improves sleep architecture and morning stiffness. Sedation is the dose-limiting side effect; most patients tolerate low bedtime doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest pain and sleep improvement; better tolerated than amitriptyline at low doses (Tofferi 2004 meta-analysis).

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose naltrexone (4.5 mg at bedtime)
medicalTreatments.description: Off-label use of a low-dose opioid antagonist hypothesized to modulate microglial activation. Evidence comes from small randomized trials. Generally well-tolerated; mild insomnia or vivid dreams in some patients. Cannot be used with concurrent opioids.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 30-40% of patients report meaningful symptom improvement in small randomized trials (Younger 2013).

medicalTreatments.name: Tramadol (25-50 mg up to four times daily, with or without acetaminophen)
medicalTreatments.description: Weak opioid with SNRI activity. Used cautiously when other options have failed. Risks include serotonin syndrome (when combined with SNRIs), dependence, and seizures. Not first-line.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest pain reduction in placebo-controlled trials; long-term data limited.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tai chi (Yang style, 60 min twice weekly for 12-24 weeks)
alternativeTreatments.description: Wang et al. NEJM 2018 randomized trial showed tai chi was equivalent to or better than aerobic exercise for fibromyalgia symptoms, with sustained benefit at 52 weeks. Strongest evidence among mind-body interventions.

alternativeTreatments.name: Yoga (gentle or hatha, 60-90 min weekly)
alternativeTreatments.description: Multiple randomized trials show improvement in pain, fatigue, and mood. Avoid hot yoga and aggressive vinyasa during flares.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR, 8-week structured program)
alternativeTreatments.description: Randomized trials show modest improvements in pain catastrophizing, sleep, and quality of life. Most effective when combined with exercise and CBT.

alternativeTreatments.name: Balneotherapy and hydrotherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Warm-water exercise and spa-based protocols show short-term pain and function improvement in randomized trials. Useful for patients who cannot tolerate land-based exercise initially.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Mixed evidence; some randomized trials show short-term pain reduction. Reasonable adjunct, not stand-alone therapy.

whySeeSpecialist: A rheumatologist should be involved when diagnosis is uncertain, when another rheumatic disease is suspected or coexists, when first-line medications fail, or when symptoms are severe enough to disable. Pain medicine physicians lead care in many practices, and a multidisciplinary team adding physical therapy and pain psychology produces the best outcomes. Many patients with established uncomplicated fibromyalgia are managed effectively in primary care with periodic specialist input.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for clinicians who explicitly recognize fibromyalgia as a real condition, who use the 2016 ACR criteria, and who structure care around exercise, behavioral support, and stepwise medication. Ask whether the practice offers access to cognitive behavioral therapy and physical therapy with chronic-pain expertise. Continuity matters — fibromyalgia is a long-term relationship, not a single consultation.
preventionStrategies: Treat sleep disorders, especially obstructive sleep apnea, promptly — restoring slow-wave sleep reduces pain amplification
Address mood and anxiety disorders early, before chronic pain layers on top, through therapy and pharmacological treatment as indicated
Maintain regular aerobic and resistance exercise to preserve descending pain inhibition and reduce deconditioning
Manage stress with practical techniques — paced activity, mindfulness, and adequate recovery time
Trauma-informed care after physical or psychological injury reduces the risk of central sensitization developing
lifestyleModifications: Start a low-intensity aerobic program — walking, cycling, swimming — at 5-10 minutes per session and increase by 10% weekly to a target of 150 minutes per week
Add resistance training twice weekly at 40-60% of one-repetition maximum, progressing gradually; muscle strengthening reduces pain and fatigue scores
Prioritize sleep hygiene: fixed wake time, dark room, limited screens after 9 pm, no caffeine after midday
Use pacing strategies — break tasks into shorter blocks with planned rest, avoid the boom-and-bust cycle of overactivity followed by collapse
Maintain a balanced anti-inflammatory diet pattern (Mediterranean or DASH); evidence for specific elimination diets is weak
Limit alcohol to under 7 drinks per week; alcohol fragments sleep and worsens fatigue
Stop smoking — smokers report higher pain intensity and worse function in fibromyalgia cohorts
Track triggers and symptoms in a simple daily diary to identify personal patterns

recommended: Mediterranean-style eating with vegetables, fruit, whole grains, legumes, and olive oil — modest evidence for pain reduction
Fatty fish twice weekly (salmon, sardines, mackerel) for omega-3 intake
Adequate vitamin D from sunlight and food; supplement if 25-hydroxy vitamin D is below 30 ng/mL
Adequate hydration — 2 liters daily unless restricted
Magnesium-rich foods (nuts, seeds, leafy greens) — evidence is modest but the macronutrient gap is common
avoid: Heavy ultra-processed food and refined sugar — observational evidence links to higher symptom severity
Alcohol in quantities that disrupt sleep (typically over 1-2 drinks)
Caffeine after midday in patients with sleep disturbance
Restrictive elimination diets without clinician guidance — they can reduce nutritional intake without measurable symptom benefit
Mega-dose supplements promoted as cures — credible evidence for specific cures does not exist

exerciseGuidelines: Aerobic, resistance, and aquatic exercise all show benefit in randomized trials. Start at 50-60% of perceived capacity and increase intensity by no more than 10% weekly. A typical starting prescription is 5-10 minutes of walking or stationary cycling 3-5 days per week, progressing toward 30-60 minutes most days. Resistance training 2 days per week and aquatic exercise in warm water suit patients who cannot tolerate land-based programs initially.
dailyManagement: Take medications at consistent times daily; bedtime dosing of amitriptyline or cyclobenzaprine should allow 8 hours before waking to avoid grogginess
Move every day — even 10 minutes of gentle activity prevents deconditioning during flares
Track symptoms, sleep, and triggers in a brief daily log for review at clinic visits
Use pacing — break tasks into manageable blocks with planned recovery rather than pushing through to exhaustion
Maintain social connection; isolation worsens pain and mood
Communicate flexibly with employers about accommodations such as ergonomic setup, paced work, and remote-work options
prognosis: Fibromyalgia is chronic and fluctuating, but lifespan is normal and meaningful symptom control is achievable for most patients. Roughly 25% achieve substantial improvement within 2 years of starting comprehensive multimodal treatment, another 50% achieve moderate improvement, and 25% have persistent severe symptoms despite optimal therapy. Without treatment, about 30% develop significant long-term disability with reduced workforce participation and lower quality of life. Predictors of better outcome include earlier diagnosis, engagement in exercise and behavioral therapy, adherence to medication, addressing comorbid depression and sleep disorders, and avoidance of opioids. Worse outcomes correlate with longer pre-diagnosis delay, untreated mood and sleep disorders, ongoing psychosocial stressors, and a passive treatment model focused only on medication. Functional outcomes — work, family, social life — improve more reliably than pain scores alone, which is why measurement should include quality of life, not just pain intensity.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients begin to notice modest symptom improvement within 4-12 weeks of starting a structured multimodal program, with maximum benefit appearing over 6-12 months. Medications typically show effect within 2-6 weeks at therapeutic dose. Exercise benefits build over 8-12 weeks of consistent practice. Symptom flares triggered by stress, illness, or overactivity remain part of the long-term pattern even in well-controlled disease, and self-management skills allow most patients to ride them out without losing overall gains.
complications: Chronic disability — roughly 30% of untreated patients leave the workforce or shift to reduced hours; appropriate treatment substantially lowers this rate
Comorbid depression and anxiety, which both worsen pain perception and impair adherence to treatment
Opioid dependence when patients are inappropriately treated with long-term opioids; tapering is difficult and rarely improves pain
Deconditioning and secondary musculoskeletal pain from inactivity, creating a self-reinforcing pain cycle
Misdiagnosis and missed comorbidity — symptoms attributed to fibromyalgia can mask new-onset rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, hypothyroidism, or sleep apnea
Social isolation and family strain from chronic invisible illness

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that informs fibromyalgia management standards.

supportResources.name: National Fibromyalgia Association
supportResources.description: US patient advocacy organization offering education, peer support, and research updates.

supportResources.name: Arthritis Foundation — Fibromyalgia
supportResources.description: Patient resources, exercise programs, and clinical updates.

supportResources.name: Support Fibromyalgia Network
supportResources.description: Community-driven patient organization with peer-led support groups.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ME/CFS centers on post-exertional malaise — a sustained worsening of symptoms 24-72 hours after even mild exertion — and profound fatigue, with widespread pain a secondary feature. Fibromyalgia centers on widespread pain with fatigue secondary. The two overlap clinically and can coexist, but exercise prescriptions differ markedly: graded aerobic exercise helps fibromyalgia and can worsen ME/CFS.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypothyroidism produces fatigue, cognitive slowing, weight gain, cold intolerance, dry skin, and diffuse muscle aching that closely mimic fibromyalgia. The decisive distinction is the TSH blood test — elevated in hypothyroidism, normal in fibromyalgia. Treating thyroid disease resolves the mimicking symptoms; persistent symptoms after TSH normalization may indicate coexisting fibromyalgia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polymyalgia Rheumatica
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polymyalgia rheumatica causes shoulder and hip-girdle pain and stiffness in adults over 50, with markedly elevated ESR and CRP and dramatic response to low-dose prednisone within 48-72 hours. Fibromyalgia has normal inflammatory markers, affects a broader age range, and does not respond to corticosteroids.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rheumatoid arthritis causes symmetric inflammatory joint swelling, prolonged morning stiffness over an hour, and positive rheumatoid factor or anti-CCP antibodies with elevated inflammatory markers. Fibromyalgia produces widespread pain without objective joint swelling or laboratory inflammation. The two coexist in roughly 20% of RA patients, complicating disease-activity assessment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus produces multi-system involvement — rashes, kidney disease, cytopenias, serositis — and positive ANA at significant titer with characteristic autoantibodies. Fibromyalgia is single-system (central pain processing) without organ involvement or specific autoantibodies. Lupus patients frequently develop concomitant fibromyalgia.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS)
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares mechanisms of central sensitization, autonomic dysregulation, and post-exertional symptom amplification; coexists in 30-70% of patients in some series.

relatedConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares central sensitization mechanisms; co-occurs in 30-50% of fibromyalgia patients.

relatedConditions.name: Migraine
relatedConditions.relevance: Tension-type headaches and migraines are present in 40-70% of fibromyalgia patients and share central sensitization pathways.

relatedConditions.name: Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-occurs in roughly 30% of fibromyalgia patients; treating depression improves pain and function.

relatedConditions.name: Polymyalgia Rheumatica
relatedConditions.relevance: Important differential in older adults with widespread musculoskeletal pain; inflammatory markers and corticosteroid response distinguish the two.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Mimicking condition that must be excluded with TSH; can also coexist and amplify fibromyalgia symptoms.

faqs.question: Is fibromyalgia a real condition?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fibromyalgia is a recognized chronic pain disorder driven by altered central pain processing, classified by the International Association for the Study of Pain as nociplastic pain. Functional MRI shows objective differences in pain-processing networks compared with healthy controls. Major bodies including ACR, EULAR, and WHO recognize the diagnosis under ICD-10 code M79.7.

faqs.question: What does fibromyalgia pain feel like?
faqs.answer: Most patients describe deep, aching, or burning pain in multiple body regions — neck, shoulders, low back, hips, arms, and legs — on both sides of the body. The pain fluctuates day to day and worsens with stress, weather changes, poor sleep, or overactivity. Light touch can feel painful, and pressure on otherwise normal soft tissue is uncomfortable.

faqs.question: How is fibromyalgia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical, based on the 2016 American College of Rheumatology criteria: widespread pain across multiple body regions for at least three months, plus a Widespread Pain Index and Symptom Severity Scale score meeting threshold values. Blood tests and imaging are used to exclude conditions that mimic fibromyalgia, not to confirm it. The 1990 tender-point exam has been retired.

faqs.question: What causes fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: The core mechanism is amplified central pain processing — the nervous system perceives normal sensory input as painful and downplays its natural pain-dampening signals. Genetics, adverse childhood experiences, sleep disruption, mood disorders, and physical or emotional trauma all contribute. No single cause has been identified, and the condition is a final common pathway from many contributing factors.

faqs.question: Is fibromyalgia curable?
faqs.answer: Fibromyalgia is chronic and not curable, but it is treatable. Most patients achieve meaningful symptom control with a multimodal plan combining exercise, cognitive behavioral therapy, sleep optimization, and targeted medication. Roughly 25% of patients achieve substantial improvement within 2 years of starting comprehensive treatment, and lifespan is normal.

faqs.question: What is fibro fog?
faqs.answer: Fibro fog describes cognitive symptoms — difficulty with short-term memory, sustained attention, word-finding, and processing speed — reported by 50-80% of fibromyalgia patients. It worsens during flares and with poor sleep, fatigue, and stress. Treating sleep disorders, mood symptoms, and pain typically improves cognitive function, though some difficulty often persists.

faqs.question: What medications work best for fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: Three medications carry US FDA approval: duloxetine 30-60 mg daily, milnacipran 50 mg twice daily, and pregabalin 150-450 mg per day divided. Amitriptyline 10-50 mg at bedtime and cyclobenzaprine at bedtime are widely used off-label with strong evidence. Low-dose naltrexone is an emerging option. Roughly half of patients achieve meaningful benefit on a given medication; combination therapy is common.

faqs.question: Can exercise help fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Aerobic, resistance, and aquatic exercise all show benefit in randomized trials and are recommended first-line by EULAR 2017. Start at low intensity (5-10 minutes) and increase gradually to avoid post-exertion flares. Tai chi performed equal to or better than aerobic exercise in the Wang NEJM 2018 trial. The key principle is starting low and progressing slowly.

faqs.question: Should I avoid opioids for fibromyalgia pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Opioids are strongly discouraged. They worsen central sensitization and hyperalgesia, carry addiction and overdose risk, and have no evidence of long-term benefit in fibromyalgia. Tramadol is sometimes used cautiously when other options fail, but is not first-line. Patients on long-term opioids for fibromyalgia generally improve when guided through a structured taper.

faqs.question: Can men get fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: Yes, though women are diagnosed 3-7 times more often than men. Male patients are likely under-diagnosed because the condition is culturally framed as a womens disease and men may report fewer symptoms. Mechanisms, treatment response, and prognosis are similar across sexes. The 2016 ACR criteria reduced the female-to-male diagnostic gap compared with the 1990 tender-point criteria.

faqs.question: Is fibromyalgia hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is a strong genetic component. First-degree relatives have an 8-fold higher risk, and twin studies estimate heritability at roughly 50%. Implicated genes affect serotonin transport, catecholamine metabolism, and pain processing. Genetics alone do not cause fibromyalgia; the condition develops when a predisposed person encounters triggering stressors such as trauma, infection, or sustained stress.

faqs.question: Is fibromyalgia a disability?
faqs.answer: Fibromyalgia can qualify as a disability under US Social Security and ADA frameworks when symptoms significantly limit work or daily activities. Successful claims require documented chronicity, severity, and functional impact, ideally with objective records from a rheumatologist or pain specialist. Workplace accommodations such as ergonomic setup, paced work, flexible hours, and remote work substantially improve workforce participation.

faqs.question: How long does it take to diagnose fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: Average time from first symptoms to diagnosis is 2-3 years in US and European cohorts. Delays are longer for women of color and patients in low-resource settings. Patients are commonly misdiagnosed with depression, hypothyroidism, or rheumatoid arthritis along the way. Awareness of the 2016 ACR criteria in primary care reduces diagnostic delay.

faqs.question: Can fibromyalgia develop after COVID?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fibromyalgia onset following SARS-CoV-2 infection has been reported in cohort studies, with new diagnoses appearing weeks to months after acute illness. Post-COVID fibromyalgia overlaps clinically with post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 (long COVID) and ME/CFS. Mechanisms likely involve central sensitization triggered by infection, immune activation, and sleep disruption during recovery.

faqs.question: What is the difference between fibromyalgia and chronic fatigue syndrome?
faqs.answer: Both involve central sensitization and overlap heavily. Fibromyalgia centers on widespread pain with fatigue secondary. Chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) centers on profound fatigue and post-exertional malaise — a sustained worsening 24-72 hours after even mild exertion. Graded aerobic exercise helps fibromyalgia but can worsen ME/CFS, so the distinction guides exercise prescription.

faqs.question: Does fibromyalgia show on blood tests or scans?
faqs.answer: No. Routine blood tests and imaging are normal in fibromyalgia. Testing is done to exclude conditions that mimic the symptom cluster — thyroid disease, anemia, vitamin D deficiency, lupus, rheumatoid arthritis. Research-grade functional MRI and quantitative sensory testing show measurable differences in pain processing, but these are not routine clinical tests.

faqs.question: How much does fibromyalgia treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Generic duloxetine, amitriptyline, and cyclobenzaprine cost USD 5-25 per month in most countries. Pregabalin generic runs USD 10-40 per month; brand-name milnacipran is more expensive. Physical therapy and cognitive behavioral therapy are the larger cost drivers and benefit substantially from insurance coverage. In India and other emerging markets, generic medications and group therapy programs are widely affordable.

faqs.question: Can children have fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Juvenile fibromyalgia affects roughly 2-6% of adolescents, more often girls, with peak diagnosis ages 13-15. Symptoms mirror adult presentation: widespread pain, sleep disturbance, fatigue, and cognitive difficulty, with major impact on school attendance. Long-term follow-up shows persistence into adulthood in about 50% of cases. Treatment combines exercise, CBT, school accommodations, and selective medication.

faqs.question: Can stress trigger a fibromyalgia flare?
faqs.answer: Yes. Psychological stress, poor sleep, illness, weather changes, overactivity, and trauma all commonly precipitate flares. The underlying mechanism is amplification of central pain processing during physiological stress. Stress-management techniques — paced activity, mindfulness, cognitive behavioral therapy, and adequate sleep — reduce flare frequency.

faqs.question: When should I see a rheumatologist?
faqs.answer: See a rheumatologist when diagnosis is uncertain, when another rheumatic disease is suspected or coexists, when first-line medications fail, or when symptoms cause significant functional impairment. Many patients with established uncomplicated fibromyalgia are managed in primary care with periodic specialist input. A multidisciplinary team including physical therapy and pain psychology produces the best outcomes.

faqs.question: Does diet affect fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: Mediterranean and DASH eating patterns show modest evidence for reducing pain and improving function. Specific elimination diets (gluten-free, low-FODMAP) help some patients with coexisting irritable bowel syndrome but are not generically recommended. Mega-dose supplements promoted as cures have no credible evidence. Modest weight loss in patients with BMI above 30 improves symptom scores.

faqs.question: Will fibromyalgia get worse over time?
faqs.answer: Fibromyalgia is chronic and fluctuating rather than progressively worsening. With consistent multimodal treatment most patients stabilize or improve over years. Worsening typically reflects untreated comorbidities (sleep apnea, depression, deconditioning), inappropriate opioid use, or psychosocial stress. Targeted treatment of these factors restores trajectory.

metaDescription: Fibromyalgia: chronic widespread pain, fatigue, and fibro fog from central pain processing. Symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment reviewed by our medical board.
